text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - FOLLOW-UP NOTE
nira Maintenance Surveillance Visit (~6 Months Post-Chemotherapy Completion)

ATTENDING: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up, established patient

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei is a 53-year-old postmenopausal female with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant carrier, presenting for routine surveillance visit while on Zejula maintenance therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei returns to clinic today for her scheduled surveillance visit, approximately six months following completion of her adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and roughly three months after initiating nira maintenance. As a brief reminder of her oncologic history: she was diagnosed with bilateral HGSOC, FIGO stage IIIC, roughly one year ago, following workup initiated for abdominal distension and a CA-125 of 1,847 U/mL. She underwent primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0) and subsequently completed six cycles of carbo AUC 5 / pacli, with pacli dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 starting cycle 4 due to grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy. Following confirmation of complete clinical and radiographic response, she was initiated on Zejula maintenance at 200 mg daily, with individualized dosing selected given her weight and baseline platelet count. She developed grade 3 thrombocytopenia (nadir platelets 41 x10^9/L) at approximately week 6 of Zejula, requiring a 28-day drug hold, after which she resumed at 100 mg daily. She has now been on Zejula 100 mg daily continuously for approximately 12 weeks since resumption.

Today she reports doing quite well overall. She denies any new abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, or change in bowel habits. No dysuria or urinary frequency. No back pain or leg swelling. No new fatigue beyond what she has previously described. She has returned to full-time work as a high school biology teacher and is keeping a full schedule without significant difficulty.

With respect to nira-related side effects, she continues to report mild nausea, which she describes as occurring primarily in the morning despite taking the medication at bedtime as instructed. She rates this a 1-2/10 in severity. She uses ondansetron PRN, approximately 2-3 times per week, which provides good relief. She has implemented dietary modifications (smaller meals, avoidance of strong odors, crackers in the morning) which have helped. She also notes mild fatigue, which she describes as noticeable in the late afternoon but not interfering with work or household activities. No bruising, unusual bleeding, mucosal bleeding, or petechiae have been noted. She denies dyspnea, palpitations, or chest pain.

Regarding the peripheral neuropathy she experienced during PTX: she reports this has essentially resolved. She notices no numbness or tingling in her fingertips or toes at rest. She was very pleased to report that fine motor function has fully recovered - she is able to button clothing and handle small objects without difficulty.

She completed genetic counseling. Her daughter (age 26) has been counseled about her own inherited risk and is in the process of pursuing germline genetic testing. Ms. Osei expressed both relief and some anxiety about her daughter's testing, which was explored in today's visit.

Her most recent labs are reviewed below. CA-125 today: 9 U/mL (normal). CBC reviewed - see below.

Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed prior to today's visit showed no evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, pelvic or abdominal masses, or lymphadenopathy. Post-surgical changes stable. Complete radiographic remission maintained.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1), no fevers, no chills, no unintentional weight loss; weight stable
- GI: Mild intermittent nausea (Grade 1), well-controlled; no vomiting, no abdominal pain, no early satiety, no change in stool caliber or frequency, no rectal bleeding
- GU: No dysuria, no hematuria, no urinary frequency or urgency beyond baseline
- Neurological: Peripheral neuropathy resolved (Grade 0); no new neurologic symptoms
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, no joint swelling
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising, no mucosal bleeding, no epistaxis, no prolonged bleeding
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no dyspnea, no palpitations; hot flashes present, managed per below
- Dermatologic: No rash
- Psychiatric: Patient reports mood generally good; some anxiety related to daughter's genetic testing underway - appropriate, discussed
- Reproductive/Endocrine: Postmenopausal symptoms including vaginal dryness and occasional hot flashes; managed with topical vaginal estrogen and venlafaxine

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 36.8 C, weight 68 kg (stable), SpO2 98% on room air

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Mood appropriate. Appears stated age.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No oral ulcers or mucositis.

Neck: No lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed, no induration or hernia appreciated. No palpable organomegaly. No ascites on exam (no shifting dullness, no fluid wave). No palpable masses.

Extremities: No edema. No calf tenderness.

Skin: No petechiae. No purpura. No ecchymoses beyond small resolving bruise on left forearm (patient reports from bumping into a counter, not spontaneous). No rash.

Neurological (focused): Sensation to light touch grossly intact in bilateral distal lower extremities. Gait normal. Grip strength symmetric and normal.

Pelvic exam: Deferred today per patient preference; scheduled for next visit.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CA-125: 9 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL) - normalized, stable from 11 U/mL at last check

CBC:
- WBC: 5.1 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, mild; down from 10.8 at nira initiation but relatively stable, trending to recover)
- Platelets: 137 x10^9/L (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia; stable range over recent monthly monitoring: 128-143 x10^9/L)
- ANC: 2.4 x10^9/L (normal)

CMP:
- BUN/Cr: 14/0.82 mg/dL (normal renal function)
- LFTs: AST 22, ALT 19, AlkPhos 74, total bilirubin 0.6 (all normal)
- Electrolytes within normal limits

Note: No further grade 3 or higher hematologic toxicity since nira dose reduction to 100 mg. Platelet counts have stabilized in the Grade 1 range, which is acceptable and does not warrant further dose modification per guidelines.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (current visit, surveillance): No peritoneal implants. No ascites. No pelvic masses. Post-surgical changes stable. No new lesions. Complete radiographic remission maintained, consistent with prior imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO IIIC, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant - currently in complete remission:
Ms. Osei continues to do extremely well. Her CA-125 is 9 U/mL, stable and well within normal limits. CT imaging confirms ongoing complete radiographic remission with no evidence of disease. She is approximately 6 months out from completing chemotherapy and doing well on Zejula maintenance. This is a very favorable trajectory for a patient with stage IIIC HGSOC, reflective of her excellent surgical outcome (R0 resection) combined with the biological advantage conferred by germline BRCA1 status in the setting of platinum-based therapy. We will continue monitoring closely per protocol.

2. Zejula MAINTENANCE (100 mg daily, dose-reduced):
Patient continues on Zejula 100 mg daily, the reduced dose established following her grade 3 thrombocytopenia episode earlier in the course. She has now been at this dose continuously for approximately 12 weeks with stable counts (platelets 128-143 x10^9/L, Grade 1 range). This is an acceptable and expected chronic side effect profile at the 100 mg dose level. No further dose adjustment needed at this time. She will continue monthly CBC monitoring. Side effects remain grade 1 nausea and fatigue, both manageable. Will continue nira maintenance per SOLO1 protocol schedule, targeting a total duration of 36 months. She is tolerating this overall very well.
  - Continue Zejula 100 mg PO daily at bedtime
  - Continue ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN for nausea (may use up to once daily)
  - Monthly CBC monitoring
  - Next CA-125 in approximately 3 months

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - RESOLVED:
Grade 2 sensory neuropathy from prior PTX has now fully resolved, which is gratifying given the degree of concern at the time of dose reduction. Patient reports no residual symptoms. No active management needed. Will continue to inquire at each visit.

4. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS:
Patient is managing vaginal dryness with local topical estrogen (vaginal estradiol cream, low-dose local application). Hot flashes managed with venlafaxine, which she reports is moderately effective. These measures are deemed acceptable in the context of her cancer history given that topical/local vaginal estrogen has minimal systemic absorption. Will continue current management.

5. GENETIC COUNSELING / FAMILIAL RISK:
Ms. Osei has completed formal genetic counseling. Her 26-year-old daughter is in the process of pursuing germline BRCA1 testing. Patient expressed appropriate concern about her daughter's results. I reassured her that regardless of the result, her daughter will be well-supported with either surveillance protocols or risk-reduction options. No additional steps required from our end at this time; referral to the familial cancer clinic is in place.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Patient appears to be coping well. She has returned to full-time work and reports her quality of life as good. Some situational anxiety surrounding her daughter's genetic testing is appropriate and expected. She has support at home. No referral to oncology social work or psychiatry needed at this time; offer remains open.

SURVEILLANCE PLAN:
- Next CA-125 in approximately 3 months
- Next CT abdomen/pelvis in approximately 6 months (12-month post-chemotherapy scan)
- Clinical visit in 3 months
- Continue monthly CBC while on Zejula
- Continue nira per plan; target completion at 36 months total duration

Patient was given opportunity to ask questions and verbalized understanding of the plan. She expressed relief at continued clean imaging and normal CA-125. She remains very engaged in her care.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 35 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gynecologic Oncology
